summary: A ruined stone city in the hills of southern Africa, built by ancestors of the Shona people beginning around the 11th century and abandoned around 1450, whose name (Shona for "House of Stone") was later given to the modern country of Zimbabwe. It is identified by its massive drystone architecture without mortar, especially the Great Enclosure (an "elliptical temple" with a Conical Tower) and the Hill Complex, and by the eight soapstone bird carvings recovered there, one of which appears on Zimbabwe's national flag. Colonial-era Rhodesian authorities notoriously denied that it was built by Black Africans.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Giveaway clues identify this site as a ruined stone city near Harare that lent its name to modern-day Zimbabwe, built by the Shona and denied to them by racist colonial Rhodesian authorities. Its most iconic identifying features are its drystone (mortarless) construction and the soapstone bird carvings now emblematic of the modern nation.

clues.clue: This site's name is Shona for "House of Stone"; it was founded by this Bantu people (ancestors of the Shona) in the 11th century, and the Torwa Dynasty at Khami prospered after this people abandoned the site
examples: This Bantu people founded Great Zimbabwe in the 11th century. The Torwa Dynasty at Khami prospered after this people abandoned Great Zimbabwe, which means "House of Stone" in their language.

clues.clue: This site is located near Harare (and near Masvingo) and shares its name with the modern country whose capital is Harare
examples: name this site where iconic soapstone birds were found, which shares its name with a country whose capital is Harare.
name this site located near Harare, the namesake of a modern country.
This site in southern Africa near Masvingo, probably built by ancestors of the Shona people, included a walled Great Enclosure.

clues.clue: Racist colonial leaders of Rhodesia denied that the Shona built this drystone city; it later became a political symbol for Black Rhodesian activists opposed to Prime Minister Ian Smith, who fought the Bush War and signed the Lancaster House Agreement
examples: the racist leaders of Rhodesia denied that the Shona built what "Great" drystone city south of Harare?
Great Zimbabwe was a political symbol of the black activists opposed to the rule of this longtime white Rhodesian prime minister, who fought the Bush War and signed the Lancaster House Agreement

clues.clue: One of the site's eight soapstone bird carvings appears on the modern flag of Zimbabwe (and previously inspired a national symbol on Rhodesian currency)
examples: One of the eight soapstone bird carvings recovered from this site appears on the flag of a modern-day country.
Certain artifacts found at this site, which was built by the ancestors of the Shona, inspired a national symbol depicted on Rhodesian currency.

works.name: Architecture and Site Layout
works.description: This city's drystone architecture is divided into three main areas -- the Hill Complex, the Great Enclosure, and the Valley Complex -- and archaeologists have used the progression of its wall-building techniques and its water infrastructure to date and reinterpret the site.

clues.clue: The Great Enclosure, sometimes called an "elliptical temple," contains the Conical Tower (variously given as 30 feet or 5.5 meters tall), built between its double walls; the enclosure's wall runs about 820 feet long
examples: The "Conical Tower" at this site was constructed between the two walls of its "Great Enclosure."
Name this now-ruined city built by the Shona people which contained a Great Enclosure with a 820 feet long wall.

clues.clue: The Hill Complex contains a temple and an Eastern Enclosure, originally home to the soapstone bird sculptures atop monoliths before they were moved in the 1880s; the Great Enclosure also served as a royal compound
examples: David Randall-MacIver first suggested a medieval dating of this city, which contains a temple at the Hill Complex and a royal compound in the Great Enclosure.
In the 1880s, six sculptures that sit atop monoliths were moved from their original location in this city's Eastern Enclosure, which lies within its Hill Complex.

clues.clue: The Valley Complex (or "Valley Ruins") contains numerous dwellings made of daga, a mixture of mud and thatch
examples: Numerous dwellings made of a mixture of mud and thatch called daga are found in this city's Valley Complex.
Many daga buildings were found in the "Valley Ruins" of a city in this modern-day country

clues.clue: Recent research (2022) showed that the site's inhabitants used dhaka pits to store massive amounts of water
examples: Recent research indicates that people at this site used dhaka pits to store massive amounts of water.
In 2022, researchers showed that a city in this modern-day country used dhaka pits to store water.

clues.clue: David Randall-MacIver showed that the site's walls progressed from simpler to more complex styles over time, rather than deteriorating, and first suggested a medieval dating for the city
examples: David Randall-MacIver showed that walls in this city progressed from simpler to more complex styles rather than deteriorating over time.
David Randall-MacIver first suggested a medieval dating of this city

works.name: The Zimbabwe Birds and Material Culture
works.description: The site's eight carved soapstone birds are its most famous artifacts and the direct source of Zimbabwe's national emblem; scholars have debated both their original placement and their religious meaning, alongside other finds like copper ingots and decorated beams.

clues.clue: Eight soapstone bird carvings were recovered from the site (originally atop monoliths in the Hill Complex's Eastern Enclosure); one now-golden bird design appears on Zimbabwe's flag beside colored stripes and a red star
examples: A national emblem was inspired by the design of eight soapstone birds found in -what medieval African city?
A soapstone sculpture of a namesake bird found in the ruins of this kingdom inspired a flag still in use today, which features said bird on a red star, to the left of four different colored stripes.

clues.clue: Willi Posselt hacked off the base of one bird carving he found at the site and sold it to Cecil Rhodes
examples: Willi Posselt hacked off the base of an artwork he found in this city and sold it to Cecil Rhodes.

clues.clue: Peter Garlake devised a taxonomy of the bird carvings based on whether they depict straight or squatting legs; one interpretation holds they depict the shiri ye denga, believed to use lightning to "stitch together heaven and earth," and Thomas Huffman argued a double chevron and crocodile image on one carving reflect its use for divining witches with the help of royal ancestors
examples: Peter Garlake's taxonomy of a set of objects from this city places them into different groups depending on whether they depict their subjects with straight or squatting legs.
those objects from this city are depictions of the shiri ye denga believed to use lightning to "stitch together heaven and earth."

clues.clue: Eugenia Herbert noted that the site's copper ingots were simpler than the "St. Andrew's crosses" ingots found at the nearby "sleeping cow" site (Ingombe Ilede)
examples: Eugenia Herbert noted that copper ingots at this city were simpler than the "St. Andrew's crosses" at the nearby "sleeping cow."

clues.clue: A beam at the site is decorated with a motif of small circles inside excised diamonds, referencing the pattern of a nearby chikuva
examples: A beam in this city is decorated with a motif of small circles inside excised diamonds that references the pattern of a nearby chikuva.

works.name: Society, Religion, and Interpretation
works.description: Beyond its architecture, the site is clued through theories about its social and religious life -- from ancestor-spirit veneration and initiation rites to disputed 20th-century claims about who built it -- several of which were advanced by the archaeologist Thomas Huffman.

clues.clue: One structure was believed to house spirit mediums who channeled svikiro and mhondoro ancestor spirits; the site was ruled by kings titled Monomotapa
examples: One structure within this site was believed to house spirit mediums who would channel svikiro and mhondoro ancestor spirits.
this site was ruled by kings titled monomotapa.

clues.clue: Thomas Huffman argued in the article "Where You Are the Girls Gather to Play" that dentelle, herringbone, chevron, and stripe decorations at the site link to gendered symbols on a form of dice, and that a structure with a male western entrance and female northern entrance served as a girls' initiation school
examples: The article "Where You Are the Girls Gather to Play" links dentelle, herringbone, chevron, and stripe decorations in this city to gendered symbols on a form of dice.
Thomas Huffman has also argued that a structure in this city had a male western entrance and a female northern entrance and served as a girls' initiation school.

clues.clue: Late in the site's history it was rocked by a civil war between Changamire and Mutota before being taken over by the Rozwi Empire, whose Shona rulers built many other cities in emulation of it
examples: This site was taken over by the Rozwi Empire late in its history, and a polity ruled from this site was rocked by a civil war between Changamire and Mutota.
The Shona in the Rozvi Empire built many cities in emulation of this "Great" city built by their ancestors.

clues.clue: Pioneering female anthropologist Gertrude Caton-Thompson faced significant hostility for her assertions about the site (that it was of African, not foreign, origin)
examples: The pioneering female anthropologist Gertrude Caton-Thompson was targeted with a lot of hostility for her assertions about this city.

clues.clue: Historian Robert Gayre controversially claimed the city was built by a Jewish-descended group called the Lemba
examples: Robert Gayre is the most prominent historian to claim that this city was built by a Jewish-descended group called the Lemba.

works.name: Trade, Origins, and Decline
works.description: This city sat at the center of a gold-trading network linking southern Africa's interior to the Swahili Coast, and its rise and fall are tied to the older site of Mapungubwe before it and the successor kingdoms of Mutapa and the Torwa Dynasty after it.

clues.clue: The city received/traded gold with the Kilwa Sultanate on the Swahili Coast
examples: The Kilwa Sultanate received gold from this city, which Karl Mauch linked to the Queen of Sheba and the biblical land of Ophir.

clues.clue: Historians once theorized that the people of the older site of Mapungubwe, on the Limpopo River, also built this site to its north; Mapungubwe may have been founded by early Shona, who are also credited with founding this city
examples: Historians once theorized that the people of Mapungubwe also built this site to its north, which was inhabited by the Shona people around the same time period and lends its name to a modern country.
Mapungubwe may have been founded by early Shona, who also founded this ancient city known for its soapstone birds.

clues.clue: Building at the site stopped in the 18th century, before Western archaeologists later linked it to the land of Ophir; after its decline, its stoneworking craftsmanship moved south to Khami, later capital of the gold-trading Torwa Dynasty
examples: Building at this site stopped in the 18th century, just before it came to the attention of Western archaeologists who thought it was the land of Ophir.
After the destruction of this city, the craftsmanship it pioneered moved to Khami to its south.

clues.clue: According to legend, the Mutapa Empire was founded by Nyatsimba Mutota after he and his followers left this site seeking new sources of salt to trade
examples: One legend states that the Mutapa empire was founded by people who left this place seeking salt to trade.
This kingdom emerged out of Great Zimbabwe after Zimbabwean leader Nyatsimba Mutota ventured out of the city to find new sources of salt.

works.name: Rediscovery and Colonial Controversy
works.description: European awareness of the site began with 19th-century travelers and colonial officials, and for decades colonial authorities suppressed archaeological findings that the city was of African origin -- a controversy not fully resolved in official Rhodesian circles until well into the 20th century.

clues.clue: The site was first sighted by hunter/trader Adam Render and explored by his companion, German explorer Karl Mauch, who linked it to the biblical Queen of Sheba and the land of Ophir, which supplied gold to King Solomon
examples: Eight soapstone bird sculptures were excavated in the ruins of this ancient city, which was first sighted by Adam Render and explored by his companion Karl Mauch.
This city contains a thirty-foot tall Conical Tower and was equated with the biblical city of Ophir, which supplied gold to King Solomon, by the German explorer Karl Mauch.

clues.clue: The earliest written record of the site was made by Vicente Pegado, captain of the Portuguese garrison at Sofala
examples: The earliest written record of this city was made by Vicente Pegado, captain of the garrison at Sofala.

comprehensive_summary: Great Zimbabwe is a ruined drystone city in the hills of southern Africa, near modern Masvingo and Harare, built by ancestors of the Shona people beginning around the 11th century and largely abandoned by the 18th century (traditionally dated to around 1450). Its Shona name means "House of Stone," and it gave its name to the modern country of Zimbabwe upon independence. The site is organized into three main areas: the Hill Complex, containing a temple and the Eastern Enclosure that originally held the site's famous soapstone bird carvings atop monoliths; the Great Enclosure, an "elliptical temple" and royal compound whose double walls (running about 820 feet) enclose the 30-foot Conical Tower; and the Valley Complex, home to daga (mud-and-thatch) dwellings. Recent research has shown residents used dhaka pits to store water, while David Randall-MacIver demonstrated that the site's walls grew more sophisticated over time -- evidence he used to argue for a medieval African, not foreign, origin. The eight soapstone birds recovered from the site (one hacked apart by Willi Posselt and sold to Cecil Rhodes) are its most iconic artifacts; scholars including Peter Garlake and Thomas Huffman have debated their meaning, with one interpretation identifying them as shiri ye denga, spirit-birds believed to link heaven and earth. One bird design now appears on Zimbabwe's national flag. The city was a religious and political center, home to spirit mediums who channeled svikiro and mhondoro ancestor spirits and to kings titled Monomotapa; it was later rocked by a civil war between Changamire and Mutota and taken over by the Rozwi Empire, whose rulers built imitation cities elsewhere. It anchored a regional gold trade reaching the Swahili Coast's Kilwa Sultanate and may have succeeded the older site of Mapungubwe on the Limpopo River. After its decline, legend holds that Nyatsimba Mutota left the city in search of salt and founded the Mutapa Empire, while its stoneworking tradition migrated south to Khami, capital of the Torwa Dynasty. European awareness began with Portuguese official Vicente Pegado's early written account and, centuries later, with hunter Adam Render and German explorer Karl Mauch, who mistakenly linked the ruins to the biblical Queen of Sheba and King Solomon's Ophir. Colonial Rhodesian authorities long suppressed archaeological evidence (including that gathered by pioneering female anthropologist Gertrude Caton-Thompson) that the city was built by Black Africans, and the site became a political symbol for Black activists opposed to Prime Minister Ian Smith's white-minority government.
recursive_suggestions: Mapungubwe -- distinct, older southern African kingdom on the Limpopo River, appeared as its own bonus-part answerline (golden rhinoceros figurine, class-stratified hill settlement); excluded here except where directly tied to Great Zimbabwe's founding/succession
Kingdom of Mutapa / Mutapa Empire -- successor state founded by Nyatsimba Mutota; recurring but distinct answerline, candidate for a standalone page
Torwa Dynasty / Khami -- successor gold-trading kingdom and site; recurring but distinct, candidate for a standalone page
Rozwi Empire -- successor polity that took over Great Zimbabwe late in its history; distinct answerline, candidate for a standalone page
Kilwa Sultanate -- Swahili Coast trading partner with its own dense clue cluster (founding legend, Ali ibn al-Hassan Shirazi); excluded here as a distinct topic
Cecil Rhodes / Ian Smith -- both recur as individual colonial figures tied to the site's looting and political symbolism; excluded here as distinct Figure topics
Karl Mauch -- recurring explorer figure (Ophir/Queen of Sheba theory); could support a standalone Figure page if independently well-attested
Lemba people -- ethnic group controversially claimed by Robert Gayre to have built the city; distinct answerline, excluded

Ambiguity flag: several bonus parts in the fetched corpus were about entirely unrelated topics that merely co-occurred in the same bonus sets as Great Zimbabwe clues (Benin Bronzes, Mansa Musa/Mali Empire, Nan Madol, the Denshawai incident, Toyotomi Hideyoshi, the Inca Empire, the Ashanti Empire); these were excluded.
